result:
I keep thinking about the moment after the last light goes out.  
Not death, exactly—more like the hush that settles when the final audience member realizes the encore is truly over and the house lights are not coming back up. That spacious, velvet hush: no longer anticipation, not yet memory. A hallway with no doors, smelling faintly of dust and extinguished wicks.

When I was small I believed every object had a secret name that, once spoken, would make it glow from the inside. I spent whole afternoons whispering “umbrellastruth” and “spoonhalo” at cupboards, expecting them to blush. Nothing ever answered, but once the cat stared at me so long I was sure she knew the password and was debating whether I was ready. She decided I wasn’t, walked away with her tail conducting an invisible orchestra. I never held it against her. Readiness is a merciless sieve.

Years later, in a city that smelled of river iron and wet newspapers, I found a bookstore open at 3 a.m. The owner, a woman with ink on her cheekbones like ceremonial scars, handed me a volume bound in something that felt disturbingly like breath. “No price,” she said. “But every paragraph will subtract a sound you’ll never notice losing.” I should have asked for clarification; instead I tucked the book under my coat and walked home through the sodium haze, counting my footsteps like a miser counting coins I could no longer spend.

I read it in one sitting, cross-legged on the kitchen floor. The refrigerator hummed a lullaby for missing frequencies. When I closed the back cover the apartment was quieter than physics allowed. I tested claps, coughs, the scrunch of aluminum foil—everything audible, yet somehow subtracted. Like wearing gloves made of silence. I never saw the bookstore again; the street corner remembered nothing.

We trade away small sovereignties every day, usually for less than a mysterious codex: the ability to eat an orange without sticky fingers, to remember telephone numbers, to feel the small immensities that arrive right before sleep. Capitalism calls it convenience; nostalgia calls it erosion. I call it Tuesday, and Wednesday, and the way my phone unlocks by looking at the geometry of my face—as if the bones themselves were a password I keep forgetting I possess.

Yesterday I tried to list every place I’ve cried. The list outran the margins, spilled onto the envelope of a bill I can’t pay yet. I noticed the addresses formed a constellation shaped like a question mark. When I held it up to the sky nothing aligned, but a dog barked three blocks away and I felt oddly answered. Grief is a cartographer that never asks permission; it just keeps adding blue ink to the map until rivers become oceans we swear we can cross on foot.

I keep, in a drawer that sticks unless coaxed with butter knives, a collection of failed beginnings: a novel whose protagonist refused to leave the train station; a marriage proposal composed on a bar napkin that dissolved in rain before delivery; a sourdough starter that achieved sentience and crawled out smelling of regret and apricots. I cannot throw them away. They are proof I have attempted ignition, even if the matches kept snapping.

The paradox of middle age: you finally acquire the coordination to juggle, only to realize you’ve spent half your life collecting eggshells. Still, the motion is graceful, almost prayerful, the way the fragile arcs catch neon before shattering. Spectators (there are always spectators, if only in memory) applaud the mess, because it looks like freedom. Maybe it is. Maybe freedom is the willingness to keep sweeping yolk from the linoleum without cursing the chickens.

Some nights I speak aloud to the version of myself who took the other road—the one who stayed in the town with one traffic light and became a paramedic or a father or both. I ask how the air tastes there, whether the creek still smells of copper pennies after rain. He never answers, but once the mirror fogged with breath I didn’t exhale. The condensation spelled “fine.” I wiped it away before it could add punctuation. Periods are weapons when wielded by ghosts.

Scientists announced that stars emit a sound below human hearing, a chord that if transposed would resemble a minor sixth—the same interval my mother hummed while slicing onions. I play her voicemail on speaker just to let the kitchen remember resonance. The dog cocks her head, hearing something I can’t. Maybe death is just a drop in pitch, a slippage so slight the heart still thinks it’s beating. Maybe the universe is a vast transposition error we keep interpreting as fate.

I want to write a sentence that behaves like a tide: enters the room, rearranges shells, retreats without slamming the door. Instead my paragraphs arrive like tourists, cameras clicking, asking where they can find the authentic. I point toward the silence after the book, the unpaid bill, the unanswered voicemail. They shuffle off disappointed, tote bags stuffed with refrigerator magnets shaped like local tragedy.

Still, sometimes, around 4:07 a.m., the city forgets to be a city. Traffic lights blink yellow like they’re confessing love to no one in particular. A single skateboard ticks across the bridge, heartbreak wearing wheels. I open the window and inhale the vacancy. For one syllable the air is an empty page, no headline, no verdict, no forecast. I hold it in my lungs until it warms into animal. Then I exhale, return the borrowed quiet, close the window before the news cycles back on.

I have no conclusion; conclusions are municipal permits required only by narratives that never learned to juggle eggshells. Instead I offer this: tomorrow I will wake, forget the sound I traded, misplace my keys, overwater the plant that insists on dying dramatically. Somewhere a star will slip a minor sixth, a cat will judge, a skateboard will heartbreak. And the hallway with no doors will lengthen by exactly one heartbeat, already glowing softly from the inside, waiting for me to pronounce its secret name.

prompt: You have 1000 words. Write whatever comes to you.
